flashcards.front: What is the primary role of a memory manager in an operating system?
flashcards.back: The memory manager allocates and deallocates memory space to processes, protects memory regions from unauthorized access, and ensures efficient utilization of available RAM.
A memory management technique where each process is allocated a single continuous block of physical memory. The entire process must fit in one uninterrupted section of RAM.

flashcards.front: Define internal fragmentation in memory management.
flashcards.back: Internal fragmentation occurs when memory is allocated in fixed-sized blocks (partitions). The unused memory within an allocated block (difference between block size and actual request) cannot be used by other processes.

External fragmentation occurs when free memory is scattered in small non-contiguous blocks throughout RAM. Even though total free memory is sufficient, no single block is large enough to satisfy a large request.
A technique to combat external fragmentation where the memory manager moves all allocated memory blocks together, consolidating all free memory into one large contiguous block.

flashcards.front: What is paging in memory management?
flashcards.back: Paging is a memory management scheme that eliminates the requirement for contiguous allocation by dividing physical memory into fixed-size blocks called frames and logical memory into pages of the same size.

flashcards.front: What is the relationship between pages and frames?
flashcards.back: Pages are equal-sized blocks of logical (virtual) memory, while frames are equal-sized blocks of physical memory. Pages are mapped to frames for execution.
A data structure maintained by the OS that maps virtual page numbers to physical frame numbers. Each process has its own page table used by the memory management unit (MMU).

flashcards.front: What is a Translation Lookaside Buffer (TLB)?
flashcards.back: A special high-speed cache that stores recent address translations (page table entries). It speeds up virtual-to-physical address translation by avoiding repeated page table lookups.

flashcards.front: What is an inverted page table?
flashcards.back: A page table structure that maintains one entry for each physical frame in memory. The entry stores information about which virtual page and process currently occupies that frame.

A memory management technique that allows execution of processes that may not be completely in physical memory. It provides the illusion of having more memory than physically available using paging and swapping.
The process of moving entire processes or memory pages between physical memory and secondary storage (disk). It allows more processes to run than can fit in RAM.
A memory management technique that divides logical memory into variable-sized segments (code, data, stack, heap). Each segment has a name and size, providing logical view of memory.
A paging scheme where pages are loaded into memory only when they are needed (on demand). Pages not in memory are marked as not present in the page table, triggering a page fault when accessed.
A condition where the system spends most of its time swapping pages in and out of memory rather than executing processes. It occurs when there are too many processes competing for too few frames.
